text: However, I understand exactly where you're coming from.  When I first started working at gamestop, and there weren't many customers in the shop at the moment, I asked one of the managers what her favorite indie games were, saying mine were minecraft and the binding of isaac. author: @Jemmysponz

text: well, as you said, they're made by a company, so technically they aren't "indie" games.  However, they also could be considered "independent" due to the fact that only a couple of people are involved in making the game.  Minecraft has several different coders, a publicity worker, someone who makes the sounds, and someone who makes the textures.  It's much more of a team effort than say, The Binding of Isaac and Super Meat Boy are.

text: What makes cave story art and minecraft not art is that Cave Story was made by one individual person who made making the game the purpose of his life just to express himself and allow the world around him to feel the way he did. All minecraft's developers ever do nowadays is add new mobs or update redstone functionality, whereas Pixel's time was spent writing the game's beautiful music or manipulating the story into something heartwarming and enjoyable. Besides, Minecraft isn't indie anymore.
author: @Jemmysponz

text: I know exactly what you mean. It's a little sad how people only think of Minecraft when they hear the term "indie game."  Because most indie games aren't that good, the truly beautiful ones like VVVVVV or Braid or To The Moon get ignored after the developer pours their very personality into it all.  However, we can at least be grateful that we noticed these masterpieces and can see them for what they're worth.  I'd even rank this game as better than any Call of Duty game I've ever seen.

text:
It's amazing games such as this, Cave Story, Tower Of Heaven, and other indie games (not including Minecraft) that are literal works of art. They proves that a fair challenge, great sound track, and/or deep stories/meanings are what define a true video game that we will always come back to play and enjoy, much like the classic games of the late '80s, '90s, and early 2000s.
But alas, in this generation where all that matters is graphics and multiplayer, such a philosophy is lost.
